title: Geometric Decoherence Time in Lindbladian Dynamics
authors: Rishabh Jha, Stephan Haas, Abhinav Prem
abstract: The onset of decoherence in open many-body systems lacks a dynamical timescale grounded in the loss of bipartite entanglement. Here, we introduce the $geometric$ $decoherence$ $time$, defined as the earliest moment the monotone relation between logarithmic negativity and Rényi-$\tfrac{1}{2}$ entropy -- exactly equal across any bipartition for pure states -- breaks down under open-system evolution, signaling entropy growth without accompanying entanglement growth. We establish this criterion in both single-particle Gaussian dynamics and many-body Lindbladian evolution. We show that quantum mutual information provides a complementary long-time diagnostic: its asymptotic vanishing is equivalent to factorization of the steady state across the bipartition, a condition strictly stronger than separability, and whenever a product steady state is approached exponentially in trace norm, negativity and mutual information share the same decay rate. In the presence of a strong symmetry, this tracking can fail -- residual classical correlations can survive after entanglement has vanished. In the Kitaev chain with balanced gain and loss, we derive a closed-form solution and show that the topological phase sustains longer coherence times than the trivial phase at identical dissipation, with a local minimum at the chiral-symmetric point. In the interacting XXZ chain, exact many-body evolution shows that local $Z$-dephasing preserves residual classical correlations, whereas gain and loss restore the mutual-information tracking of negativity. Our results establish the geometric decoherence time as a dynamical scale tracking the onset of decoherence.
summary: This paper introduces the geometric decoherence time ($	au_{g}^{	ext{d}}$) as a precise measure for the onset of decoherence in open quantum systems. By analyzing the breakdown of monotonicity between entanglement measures and entropy, the authors demonstrate that this timescale is sensitive to underlying physics, such as topological order in the Kitaev chain. It provides a powerful tool for characterizing the transition from coherent quantum dynamics to a classical steady state.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of defining a clear dynamical timescale for the onset of decoherence in open many-body systems, where entanglement loss and entropy growth occur concurrently.
main_result: The geometric decoherence time ($	au_{g}^{	ext{d}}$), defined by the breakdown of a monotone relation between logarithmic negativity and Rényi entropy, is established as a robust diagnostic scale for decoherence onset.
method: The authors analyze the time evolution of quantum correlations using the Lindblad master equation, deriving specific criteria based on the monotonicity of entanglement measures.
model_or_system: The study applies to open many-body systems, including the Kitaev chain and the interacting XXZ spin chain, evolving under Lindbladian dynamics characterized by gain and loss jump operators.
key_observables: Logarithmic Negativity ($	ext{EN}$), Rényi-1/2 Entropy ($S_{1/2}$), Quantum Mutual Information ($I(A:B)$), and the geometric decoherence time ($	au_{g}^{	ext{d}}$).
important_parameters: Dissipation rates ($\gamma$), system parameters ($\mu, J, \Delta$), and the comparison between topological and trivial phases.
main_assumptions: The geometric criterion is assumed to hold for both single-particle Gaussian dynamics and interacting Lindbladian evolution, though symmetry can cause tracking failures.
figures_summary: Figures compare $	au_{g}^{	ext{d}}$ against $	au_{	ext{peak}}^{	ext{d}}$ for the Kitaev chain, showing topological protection, and illustrate the time evolution of $	ext{EN}$ and $I(A:B)$ for different dissipation protocols in the XXZ chain.
paper_structure: The paper develops the geometric criterion ($	au_{g}^{	ext{d}}$) by analyzing the entropy-negativity trajectory, applies this to the Kitaev chain to show topological protection, and extends the analysis to the interacting XXZ chain to distinguish between symmetry-protected classical correlations and true decoherence.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous, operational measure ($	au_{g}^{	ext{d}}$) to quantify when a quantum system effectively loses its quantum coherence due to environmental coupling, which is crucial for understanding quantum computation and thermalization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Decoherence-time landscape for the balanced gain/loss Kitaev chain in the (µ, γ) plane. Left: geometric decoherence time τ g d(µ, γ). Center: peak-based estimate τ peak d (µ, γ). Right: point-by-point comparison, with dashed diagonal τ peak d = τ g d. Vertical guide lines at µ = ±2J mark the closed-system topological phase boundaries. The topological phase |µ| < 2J sustains longer τ g d than the trivial phase, and within the topological phase τ g d shows a local suppression at the chiral-symmetric point µ = 0 and decreases again toward |µ| = 2J. The comparison panel shows that τ peak d is correlated with τ g d, while exhibiting qualitatively significant discrepancies in the...

Figure 2: Geometric decoherence time in the topological phase of the open Kitaev chain (µ = 0.5, J = ∆= 1, L = 128, LA = 11, balanced gain/loss γ = 0.15, N´eel initial state). Left: time evolution of EF (fermionic Gaussian negativity), S1/2(A), and I(A:B) under unitary dynamics (dashed) and Lindbladian dynamics (solid); the vertical marker indicates τ g d. Right: parametric trajectory Γ(t) = (S1/2, EF ) colored by time; triangles denote unitary dynamics, circles denote Lindbladian dynamics. Under unitary dynamics, the trajectory is pinned to the diagonal; under Lindbladian dynamics it bends away at τ g d (square marker). At late times EF and I(A:B) both vanish, signaling asymptotic...

Figure 3: Mutual information I(A:B) and fermionic Gaussian negativity EF as functions of time for the same parameters as Fig. 2. The two quantities share the same peak and trough times as well as the same asymptotic decay rate, consistent with Theorem 1 (see Sec. II C). Exponential fits over the second half of the time window yield λEF ≈λI ≈0.50, confirming a shared decay expo- nent to two significant figures.

Figure 4: Fixed-µ comparison within the topological phase. Left: topological reference point, µ = 0.50 and γ = 0.15. Right: weaker dissipation, µ = 0.50 and γ = 0.02. The weak-dissipation panel shows the clear overshoot of τ peak d relative to τ g d where the peak is at- tained at much later time and is comparatively flat. In both panels τ g d is extracted from Definition 1 via the entropy–negativity trajectory, which is not shown here.

Figure 5: Time evolution of exact logarithmic negativity EN and von Neumann mutual information I(A:B) for the interacting XXZ chain under two dissipative protocols. (a) Under local z-dephasing (which preserves a strong U(1) symmetry), EN →0 while I(A:B) saturates to a nonzero plateau, signaling a separable but non-product NESS. The mutual-information tracking implied by Proposition 1 and Theorem 1 does not apply because the NESS does not factorize. (b) Under local gain/loss, which removes the Sz tot conservation, both EN and I(A:B) decay to zero with aligned peak and decay times, consistent with asymptotic factorization (Proposition 1 and Theorem 1). Parameters: open XXZ chain, L = 10,...
